title: Open Redirect
principle: Don't send a redirect to a URL the user supplies. Redirect only to relative paths within your own app, or map an opaque key to a known destination. If you must accept a target, allowlist the exact hosts and reject anything with its own scheme or host, including protocol-relative //evil.com and the /\evil.com form that browsers normalize to //.

snippets.secure:
from urllib.parse import urlparse
nxt = request.args.get('next', '/')
# Same-site paths only. urlparse leaves netloc empty for '/\\evil.com',
# but a browser reads the backslash as '/', so reject a leading // or /\\ too.
if urlparse(nxt).netloc or not nxt.startswith('/') or nxt[:2] in ('//', '/\\'):
    nxt = '/'
return redirect(nxt)
snippets.note: Django ships django.utils.http.url_has_allowed_host_and_scheme for exactly this. A value with a netloc (host) or one starting with // is off-site.
Reject //host and /\host (both resolve off-site) and any absolute URL. Better: pass a short key (?next=dashboard) and look up the path server-side.

snippets.library: a same-app path check before sendRedirect
snippets.note: Only redirect to app-relative paths, or an allowlist. Spring's UriComponentsBuilder plus an allowlist check works for cross-host cases.
Confirm the target is a single-slash absolute path on your own site; reject //, /\, and any parsed URL with a Host.

references.title: OWASP Unvalidated Redirects and Forwards Cheat Sheet
CWE-601: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site
